query: Retriculocytosis in different types of anemia

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Red Blood Cell -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Erythrocyte size, distribution, and shape variation can all be clues to clinical disease and pathologic processes. For example, acanthocytes may be a byproduct of eryptosis or organized erythrocyte breakdown and death. This process of tabulated cell destruction has been seen in anemia and excess calcium. Agglutination of red blood cells can be an indication of a hypercoagulable state, and microcytosis or small red blood cells can be associated with different forms of microcytic anemia, such as iron deficiency and thalassemia. [8] Additionally, red blood cell morphology has a significant role in the severity of the disease. For example, in sickle cell disease, the concentration of inherited hemoglobin S determines the degree of erythrocyte sickling.

retrieved_docs.title: Poikilocytosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Not all RBCs will take on an abnormal shape. Patients with poikilocytosis have some normally shaped cells RBCs mixed with abnormally shaped poikilocytes. Sometimes, many different types of poikilocytes are present in one patient's blood smear in conditions like iron deficiency anemia, megaloblastic anemia, etc.

retrieved_docs.title: Poikilocytosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Anisocytosis is the abnormal size of RBCs. As a general rule, hypochromic microcytosis deals with iron deficiencies (with subsequent hemoglobin deficiencies) or hemoglobinopathies, while macrocytic anemia deals with B12 and folate deficiencies or hypothyroidism (causing nuclear/cytoplasmic asynchrony in maturation). [46] [47] [48] The remaining ailments often fall within the "normochromic normocytic" category.

retrieved_docs.title: Acanthocytosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The membrane of red blood cells (RBCs) can undergo a variety of changes leading to morphologic alterations in these cells. When viewed under a freshly prepared peripheral blood smear (PBS), acanthocytes appear as cells with a few spicules of different sizes, projecting from the surface of the RBC membrane at irregular intervals. They result from alterations in membrane lipids and proteins and occur in patients with severe liver dysfunction, neuroacanthocytosis, abetalipoproteinemia, malnutrition, hypothyroidism, post-splenectomy, etc. [1] The clinical significance of acanthocytes lies in their vulnerability to splenic trapping and destruction due to their morphology, which ultimately leads to anemia. [2] When remodeled by the spleen in liver disease patients, acanthocytes develop more blunt spicules and become relatively more spherocytic; these are known as spheroacanthocytes or ‘spur cells.’

retrieved_docs.title: Myelophthisic Anemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia is reduced hemoglobin, red blood cells, or hematocrit levels below their lower normal range. There are different types and causes of anemia. Anemia is subdivided into microcytic, macrocytic, and normocytic variants. Myelophthisic anemia is categorized under the normocytic variety of anemia. Normocytic anemia has the mean corpuscular volume (MCV)  within the normal range of 80 to 100 fL. Other types of normocytic anemia apart from myelophthisic anemia include aplastic anemia, anemia of chronic disease, and anemia of renal disease. Microcytic anemia is MCV below 80 fL, including sideroblastic anemia, iron deficiency anemia, and thalassemia. The macrocytic anemias have MCV greater than 100 fL, including megaloblastic and non-megaloblastic anemia. Anemia can be asymptomatic or can present with mild to severe symptoms. Severe symptoms can be devastating since they can limit the functional capacity of the individual to carry out even basic activities of daily living. A recent article by Kassebaum NJ et al reported that about 27% of the world population is affected by anemia, with iron deficiency anemia being the most common subtype implicated. [1] Anemia considerably affects morbidity and mortality; thereby, it is of the utmost relevance in making timely diagnoses and undertaking effective treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Erythrocyte Inclusions -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders that affect erythrocytes can lead to decreased hematopoiesis, erythrocyte shape or size abnormalities, or qualitative and quantitative hemoglobin abnormalities. Erythrocyte inclusions are seen in many hematological disorders, both benign and malignant. Some of the most commonly encountered hematological disorders are anemias, which often have vague and frequently overlapping symptoms. Erythrocyte inclusions may be pathological evidence of specific diseases or disorders when interpreted within the context of presenting clinical symptoms and signs. Characterizing and identifying RBC inclusions can provide or support the diagnosis of various clinical conditions. This activity reviews the morphological characteristics, staining patterns, and clinical correlates of the most commonly encountered erythrocyte inclusions.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocytic Anemia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Nonmegaloblastic macrocytic anemia will not have hypersegmented neutrophils; instead, its PBS will show round macrocytes or macro reticulocytes, in addition to other cells representative of the underlying etiology including acanthocytes from liver disease, myeloid dysplasia, or immaturity in primary bone marrow disease, polychromatophilic RBCs with reticulocytosis, schistocytes from hemolysis, or spherocytes in inherited hemolytic anemias, eg, hereditary spherocytosis. The term "polychromasia" describes the color variation in the peripheral blood smear (PBS) from reticulocytosis. Reticulocytes have a bluish-purple-like hue, contrasting with the erythrocyte red. This contrast, this variation, is suggestive of reticulocytosis, especially when considered in a field of younger, larger red blood cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Normochromic Normocytic Anemia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The RBCs' average size and hemoglobin content are within the normal range in typical normocytic normochromic anemia cases. Under microscopic examination, RBCs usually appear similar to normal cells. However, remarkable variations in each cell size and shape may be detectable in some cases, causing normal average values. [17] Bone marrow morphology can help us differentiate various types of anemia: Normal morphology: Secondary anemia (inflammation, renal disease, renal disease, endocrine deficiency, etc) Hypoplastic: Aplastic anemia Infiltration/fibrosis: Leukemia, myelofibrosis, metastases Dyserythropoetic: Myelodysplasia

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocytic Anemia -- Etiology -- Nonmegaloblastic Anemia
retrieved_docs.content: Nonmegaloblastic anemia, which lacks hypersegmented neutrophils, occurs in various settings. Benign conditions include alcohol consumption, red blood cell (RBC) toxicity, hereditary spherocytosis (ie, impaired volume regulation increases red cell size), hypothyroidism, liver disease due to lipid deposition in the cell membrane, and marked reticulocytoses from states of excess RBC consumption, such as hemolysis or turnover in pregnancy or primary bone marrow disease (ie, reticulocytes are larger than the average RBCs).

retrieved_docs.title: Poikilocytosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Anisochromia is variability in the color density of erythrocytes due to unequal hemoglobin, including hypochromic cells seen in iron deficiency anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocytic Anemia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Megaloblastic macrocytic anemia contains hypersegmented neutrophils and macro-ovalocytes (macrocytes having an oval shape with a reduced or absent central pallor) on a peripheral blood smear (PBS) (see Image . Macrocytic Anemia). Anisocytosis and poikilocytosis are not uncommon due to ineffective erythropoiesis. Bone marrow evaluation will demonstrate hypercellularity with abnormal maturation and proliferation of myeloid cell lines, particularly the erythroid lineage.

retrieved_docs.title: Poikilocytosis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The most common types of poikilocytosis are sickle cells, target cells, spherocytes, elliptocytes, ovalocytes, echinocytes, and acanthocytes.

retrieved_docs.title: Acanthocytosis -- Clinicopathologic Correlations
retrieved_docs.content: For maintenance of fluidity of RBC morphology, membrane fluidity is essential. This fluidity is, in turn, controlled by proportions of cholesterol, structural proteins, and phospholipids in the RBC membrane. Diseases such as abetalipoproteinemia, severe liver dysfunction, etc. and neuroacanthocytosis, etc. affect the cholesterol and protein content of the RBC membrane, respectively. Subsequently, the RBC membrane fluidity becomes altered, and the cells undergo morphologic changes leading to the formation of acanthocytes or spur cells. These structural modifications make them susceptible to splenic trapping and destruction, ultimately leading to hemolytic anemia. Additionally, there has been a different hypothesis suggesting that a component of the RBC membrane skeleton, band 3 is responsible for acanthocyte formation. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Erythrocyte Inclusions -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify patients who may benefit from a peripheral blood smear to evaluate possible erythrocyte inclusions based on their clinical history. Identify the limitations of automated analyzers in the evaluation of erythrocyte inclusions. Differentiate among the most commonly encountered erythrocyte inclusions by morphological characteristics and staining patterns. Correlate specific erythrocyte inclusions with potential clinical diagnoses. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Red Blood Cell -- Microscopy, Light
retrieved_docs.content: A bite cell has a semi-circular indentation as if someone took a bite from it. Echinocytes are erythrocytes with uniform projections all equidistant apart. Schistocyte is a term that refers to an RBC fragment that has been sheared. A sickle cell is an RBC with a crescentic shape. Spherocytes are erythrocytes that have no central pallor and are uniformly red throughout. Stomatocytes have a linear central clearing rather than a circular one. Target cells have a red center with a central clearing. Teardrop cells taper on one end. One last group of erythrocyte descriptors evaluated with light microscopy is inclusion bodies. These are cytoplasmic or nuclear findings that give clinical clues. For example, Heinz bodies are small round masses seen with supravital or Heinz stains that indicate hemolysis. Howell-Jolly bodies are solid, large, round masses found in the hemoglobin of the cell, are visualized with Wright-stain, and have many clinical significances. Pappenheimer bodies are multiple, small blue inclusion bodies throughout the entire RBC, indicating iron overload, hyposplenism, or myelodysplasia. Finally, basophilic stippling is blue/purple granules seen in thalassemia, lead poisoning, and other pathologies. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Acanthocytosis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Post-splenectomy: Splenectomy leads to several morphologic changes in RBCs, including acanthocytosis.

All of the aforementioned anemia subtypes—فقر الدم الوبيل, فقر الدم اللا تنسجي, فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب, فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد, فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية—as well as التهاب المفاصل (arthritis) and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation) are listed as members of اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ), the standardized medical query for immune‑mediated or autoimmune disorders. Although فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية is defined as a non‑immune hemolytic anemia, it is still grouped within the immune‑disorder category for classification and reporting purposes, reflecting a pragmatic rather than a pathophysiological contradiction. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1800
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is identified as a specific subtype of فقر الدم (anemia), indicating that it falls under the broader category of anemia disorders. In clinical practice, فقر الدم (anemia) is frequently associated with متلازمة فانكوني (Fanconi syndrome), a renal tubular disorder that can lead to impaired reabsorption of nutrients and electrolytes, contributing to the development of anemia. Consequently, patients with Fanconi syndrome may present with various forms of anemia, including acute posthemorrhagic anemia when hemorrhagic events occur, highlighting the importance of recognizing the link between renal tubular dysfunction and hematologic abnormalities for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2004
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation of فقر دم فانكوني (Fanconi anemia), indicating that the clinical and laboratory findings associated with this genetic disorder are considered abnormal. Fanconi anemia is a hereditary condition characterized by bone‑marrow failure, congenital anomalies, and a heightened risk of malignancies, and the descriptor “Abnormal” reflects the pathological nature of its presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1709
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified within the broader category of Other deficiency anemias. The group Other deficiency anemias encompasses several specific anemia types, including فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات (iron‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (non‑megaloblastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), and فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية (autoimmune hemolytic anemia). These specific entities are considered children or sub‑classifications of Other deficiency anemias, illustrating how anemia in chronic kidney disease relates to a spectrum of deficiency‑related and hemolytic anemias and informing clinicians’ diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

retrieved_docs.content: how “Other deficiency anemias” functions as a central classification node that integrates nutritional, genetic, and hemolytic anemia subtypes while intersecting with autoimmune, metabolic, ophthalmologic, and renal disease domains. Recognizing these hierarchical relationships and clinical linkages supports comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and multidisciplinary management of patients presenting with anemia and its associated systemic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
retrieved_docs.content: The category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions, including فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (aplastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (hemolytic anemia not caused by autoimmunity), التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). These entities are grouped together because they involve immune system dysregulation, whether through autoantibody‑mediated attacks, cell‑mediated immune injury, or broader immune‑related inflammatory processes. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the classification for organizational purposes despite its different pathogenesis. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying immune component helps clinicians identify common diagnostic pathways and consider immunomodulatory therapies across different organ systems, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

Thalassemia is also associated clinically with sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية). Collectively, these entities illustrate that various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, especially those within the “other deficiency anemia” group, share immunologic associations and frequently intersect with chronic kidney disease and other immune‑mediated conditions, highlighting the need for coordinated hematologic, immunologic, and nephrologic assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1237
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is categorized under the broader group of Other deficiency anemias. The category Other deficiency anemias includes specific types such as folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات) and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات). It also has subordinate subtypes, including non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) and acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب). Recognizing that anemia associated with chronic kidney disease belongs to this classification helps clinicians consider overlapping mechanisms—such as nutrient deficiencies and impaired erythropoiesis—when evaluating and managing patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2474
retrieved_docs.content: Other deficiency anemias is a broad category of anemia that is classified as فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia) and فقر الدم الوبيل (pernicious anemia). Within this category, it has a child subgroup called فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (non‑megaloblastic anemia), and it also has a relationship with فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑type hemolytic anemia). These connections illustrate that other deficiency anemias encompass several specific forms of anemia, ranging from nutrient‑deficiency–related types to a hemolytic variant, highlighting the diversity of mechanisms and classifications relevant to diagnosis and patient management.

Within this hierarchy, non‑spherocytic anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي, also termed aplastic anemia) and acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب) are child entities, while cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد) is noted as a related condition. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1538
retrieved_docs.content: Other deficiency anemias is a broad diagnostic category that includes several specific types of anemia. It is classified as فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia) and as فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات (iron‑deficiency anemia), reflecting its inclusion of nutrient‑deficiency–related blood disorders. In addition, the category is classified as فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (non‑immune‑mediated hemolytic anemia), indicating that it also encompasses hemolytic anemias that are not caused by autoimmunity. Within this grouping, فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia) is identified as a child or subcategory, further specifying a type of hemolysis that develops secondary to external factors. Together, these relationships show that “Other deficiency anemias” serves as an umbrella term that integrates both deficiency‑based anemias (folate and iron) and certain non‑immune hemolytic anemias, including acquired forms, which is important for accurate diagnosis, classification, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 47
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia associated with chronic kidney disease is placed within the “Other deficiency anemias” group, which also comprises folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات), vitamin B12‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات). Within this category are several sub‑entities: non‑megaloblastic (aplastic) anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد), and non‑immune hemolytic anemia (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية). Although thalassemia (الثلاسيميا) is a hemoglobinopathy rather than a classic deficiency anemia, it is organized under the same “Other deficiency anemias” heading, and sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية) has been linked to thalassemia, indicating a retinal vascular complication of this hemoglobin disorder. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: disorders/autoimmune diseases (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)). Autoimmune hemolytic anemia is clinically associated with hypocalcemia (low blood calcium, نقص كالسيوم الدم), highlighting a relevant metabolic abnormality. Collectively, the classification of anemia subtypes and the detailed framework for microscopic bone‑marrow and spleen examinations provide clinicians with a structured approach to diagnose and manage hematologic disorders—particularly those related to chronic kidney disease, nutritional deficiencies, or immune‑mediated hemolysis—and to guide appropriate laboratory investigations and recognition of associated metabolic or inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2166
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified under the broader group of Other deficiency anemias. The category Other deficiency anemias encompasses several distinct subtypes, including folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات) and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات). It also includes acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب) as a child category, as well as non‑immune hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية). Recognizing that anemia in chronic kidney disease belongs to this heterogeneous group helps clinicians consider underlying nutritional deficiencies and hemolytic mechanisms when evaluating and managing affected patients.
